growth_records.medium: Minimal defined medium with glucose as sole carbon source (phosphate-buffered, pH 7)

up.method: HPLC (Aminex-87H); uptake rate = slope of [glucose] vs cell density

sec.method: HPLC; described as a minor product

mfa.note: Fig. 1 FBA flux map for C. pasteurianum; per-product relative fluxes only in figure image; absolute values in SI Appendix Table S6

growth_records.cite: Mueller, Elliott P. et al. Metabolic controls on the carbon isotope fractionations of bacterial fermentation. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America (2025). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2511040122. [PMC12541407]
growth_records.snippet: C. pasteurianum made acetate, butyrate, and a minor amount of formate. ... Growth rates for E. coli, V. fischeri, C. pasteurianum are similar at 0.47, 0.434, and 0.473 h-1.
growth_records.notes: mu=0.473 h-1 reported (OD600, SI Fig. S1). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.473 = 1.465 h. Butyrate-type clostridial fermentation of glucose; products acetate + butyrate + minor formate measured by HPLC. Strain not named in main text. Phosphate buffer pH 7. Full medium in SI Tables S1-S5 -> not stated. Absolute rates in SI Table S6; rate fields null (not invented). Growth temperature not stated in main text.

growth_records.medium: Modified Biebl (2001) production medium, glucose sole substrate

up.method: HPLC (Aminex HPX-87H); consumption rate not quantified for glucose-only run

sec.units: g/L (final titer; no natural route from glucose)

sec.method: stated qualitatively (C. pasteurianum ferments carbohydrate to butyrate, acetate, CO2, H2)

growth_records.cite: Sabra et al. Improved n-butanol production by a non-acetone producing Clostridium pasteurianum DSMZ 525 in mixed substrate fermentation. Applied Microbiology and Biotechnology (2014). DOI:10.1007/s00253-014-5588-8. [PMC3986902]
growth_records.snippet: A maximum n-butanol concentration of 6.9 g/L was obtained, and most of the glucose was directed for the production of organic acids... the two phases, acidogenesis and solventogenesis, were clearly separated during glucose fermentation
growth_records.notes: Table 1 mono-substrate glucose row (pH-controlled bioreactor, pH 6, 35C, anaerobic batch). mu=0.25 h-1 reported in Results ('specific growth rate ... in the presence of glucose ... reached 0.25 h-1'); doubling_time=ln2/mu=0.693/0.25=2.77 h. Titers (g/L): butyric 10.5, acetic 11.9, 1,3-PDO 0, butanol 6.9, biomass 13.2; butanol yield 0.1 g/g_C3+C6; overall butanol productivity 0.21 g/L/h (all Table 1). Being a classical acid producer strain produces butyrate, acetate, CO2 and H2 (Dabrock 1992 cited).

growth_records.medium: Modified Biebl production medium, spruce biomass hydrolysate (BH) sole substrate

up.method: HPLC; rate not separately quantified for BH-only run

growth_records.cite: Sabra et al. Improved n-butanol production by a non-acetone producing Clostridium pasteurianum DSMZ 525 in mixed substrate fermentation. Applied Microbiology and Biotechnology (2014). DOI:10.1007/s00253-014-5588-8. [PMC3986902]
growth_records.snippet: the growth rate on BH is slightly slower (0.2 compared to 0.25 h-1 with pure glucose)
growth_records.notes: Table 1 mono-substrate BH row. mu=0.2 h-1 reported in Discussion; doubling_time=0.693/0.2=3.47 h. Titers (g/L): butyric 11.7, acetic 15.5, 1,3-PDO 0, butanol 6.8, biomass 8.5; butanol yield 0.1 g/g; overall productivity 0.20 g/L/h. Concentrated spruce hydrolysate stock = 550 g/L glucose + 35 g/L xylose.

growth_records.medium: Modified Biebl production medium, glycerol sole substrate

up.method: HPLC; consumption rate not quantified for glycerol-only bioreactor run

sec.units: g/L (final titer; Table 1). Fig 2a control reports 15.2 g/L
g/L (final titer; Table 1). Fig 2a control reports 6.2 g/L

growth_records.cite: Sabra et al. Improved n-butanol production by a non-acetone producing Clostridium pasteurianum DSMZ 525 in mixed substrate fermentation. Applied Microbiology and Biotechnology (2014). DOI:10.1007/s00253-014-5588-8. [PMC3986902]
growth_records.snippet: without butyrate addition, the major products formed during glycerol fermentation were n-butanol (15.2 g/L) and 1,3-PDO (6.2 g/L). The production of butyric and acetic acids was negligible ... did not exceed 0.5 g/L
growth_records.notes: Table 1 mono-substrate glycerol row. mu=0.19 h-1 reported ('compared to 0.19 h-1 on pure glycerol'); doubling_time=0.693/0.19=3.65 h. Table 1 titers (g/L): butyric 0.1, acetic 0.4, 1,3-PDO 5.3, butanol 13.9, biomass 3.2; butanol yield 0.25 g/g, 1,3-PDO yield 0.1 g/g; overall butanol productivity 0.68 g/L/h. Fig 2a (pH-controlled control, same condition) reports butanol 15.2 g/L and 1,3-PDO 6.2 g/L (slightly higher; noted for transparency). Initial glycerol 25 g/L.

up.units: g/L/h; consumption unaffected by glycerol limitation (rate not numerically given for 4:1)

sec.units: g/L/h (overall productivity); dropped from 0.76 to 0.4 g/L/h after glycerol limitation
g/L (final titer; text also states max 12 g/L)

growth_records.cite: Sabra et al. Improved n-butanol production by a non-acetone producing Clostridium pasteurianum DSMZ 525 in mixed substrate fermentation. Applied Microbiology and Biotechnology (2014). DOI:10.1007/s00253-014-5588-8. [PMC3986902]
growth_records.snippet: in experiments with a glucose-to-glycerol ratio of 4:1 ... after the onset of glycerol limitation, the n-butanol productivity decreased from 0.76 to 0.4 g/L x h, and a maximum n-butanol concentration of 12 g/L was obtained
growth_records.notes: Table 1 mixed Glu:Gly 4:1 row. mu=0.25+/-0.02 h-1 (glucose-containing co-substrate); doubling_time=0.693/0.25=2.77 h. Table 1 titers (g/L): butyric 9.9, acetic 5.3, 1,3-PDO 2.2, butanol 11.3, biomass 6.8; butanol yield 0.13 g/g, 1,3-PDO 0.03 g/g; overall productivity 0.41 g/L/h. Butanol productivity fell 0.76->0.4 g/L/h at glycerol limitation (Fig 6 text); text max titer 12 g/L vs Table 11.3.

sec.units: g/L/h (overall productivity; Table 1). Max productivity 0.9-0.96 g/L/h reported in text
g/L (final titer; text 21-21.5 g/L, highest batch titer without in situ removal)

growth_records.cite: Sabra et al. Improved n-butanol production by a non-acetone producing Clostridium pasteurianum DSMZ 525 in mixed substrate fermentation. Applied Microbiology and Biotechnology (2014). DOI:10.1007/s00253-014-5588-8. [PMC3986902]
growth_records.snippet: In the fermentation with glucose-to-glycerol ratio of 1:1, where no limitation of either substrate was observed, the maximum n-butanol concentration was obtained and recorded 21 g/L ... The maximum productivity of n-butanol recorded a value
growth_records.notes: Table 1 mixed Glu:Gly 1:1 row (optimal). mu=0.25+/-0.02 h-1; doubling_time=0.693/0.25=2.77 h. Table 1 titers (g/L): butyric 4.2, acetic 1.5, 1,3-PDO 5.2, butanol 21.1, biomass 6.1; butanol yield 0.23 g/g, 1,3-PDO 0.06 g/g; overall productivity 0.69 g/L/h (Table) / max 0.9-0.96 g/L/h (text, abstract). Abstract: 21 g/L and max productivity 0.96 g/L/h.

up.units: g/L/h (initial; decreased to 2.3 g/L/h after glucose exhaustion, then stopped)

growth_records.cite: Sabra et al. Improved n-butanol production by a non-acetone producing Clostridium pasteurianum DSMZ 525 in mixed substrate fermentation. Applied Microbiology and Biotechnology (2014). DOI:10.1007/s00253-014-5588-8. [PMC3986902]
growth_records.snippet: with a glucose-to-glycerol ratio of 1:4, immediately after glucose consumption, the glycerol consumption rate decreased from 4.3 to 2.3 g/L x h and lasted for 5 h and then stopped completely. A maximum n-butanol concentration of 11 g/L was
growth_records.notes: Table 1 mixed Glu:Gly 1:4 row. mu=0.25+/-0.02 h-1 (glucose present); doubling_time=0.693/0.25=2.77 h. Measured glycerol consumption RATE 4.3->2.3 g/L/h (Fig 5 text). Table 1 titers (g/L): butyric 4.11, acetic 1.6, 1,3-PDO 10.3, butanol 11.4, biomass 5.2; butanol yield 0.17 g/g, 1,3-PDO 0.15 g/g; overall productivity 0.37 g/L/h.

growth_records.cite: Sabra et al. Improved n-butanol production by a non-acetone producing Clostridium pasteurianum DSMZ 525 in mixed substrate fermentation. Applied Microbiology and Biotechnology (2014). DOI:10.1007/s00253-014-5588-8. [PMC3986902]
growth_records.snippet: in experiments with 1:1 ratio of BH to glycerol, glucose was consumed with a rate of 3.2 g/L x h compared to a glycerol consumption rate of 2.2 g/L x h
growth_records.notes: Table 1 mixed BH:Gly 1:1 row. mu=0.2 h-1 (BH-based); doubling_time=0.693/0.2=3.47 h. Measured consumption RATES: glucose 3.2 g/L/h, glycerol 2.2 g/L/h. Table 1 titers (g/L): butyric 4.1, acetic 4.2, 1,3-PDO 1.8, butanol 17.4, biomass 6.1; butanol yield 0.2 g/g, 1,3-PDO 0.02 g/g; overall productivity 0.62 g/L/h. Abstract: BH+glycerol gave 17 g/L n-butanol at max productivity 1.1 g/L/h.

growth_records.medium: CGM (Clostridial Growth Medium) with 100 g/L crude glycerol as sole carbon source

up.method: HPLC (Fig. 2a); value derived from M150B consumption and reported ratio

growth_records.cite: Sandoval, Nicholas R et al. Whole-genome sequence of an evolved Clostridium pasteurianum strain reveals Spo0A deficiency responsible for increased butanol production and superior growth. Biotechnology for Biofuels (2015). DOI:10.1186/s13068-015-0408-7. [PMC4690370]
growth_records.snippet: In simple batch cultures without pH control and feeding or medium optimization, Cpa strain M150B showed a decreased lag phase, 65 % higher glycerol consumption (40.4 g/L), and 91 % higher butanol production (final titer 7.1 g/L) compared to
growth_records.notes: WT crude-glycerol condition. Only titers (g/L), no time-normalized rates or numeric growth rate given (specific growth rate shown only in Fig. 1 as function of crude-glycerol concentration). WT glycerol consumption derived: M150B 40.4 g/L = 65% higher than WT => WT = 40.4/1.65 = 24.48 g/L. WT butanol derived: M150B 7.1 g/L = 91% higher => WT = 7.1/1.91 = 3.72 g/L. WT PDO 2.6 g/L stated directly. Ethanol/lactate <0.5 g/L both strains both conditions. Acetate a CGM component (~1.8 g/L) partially consumed (uptake). Temp 37C, pH 6.8, anaerobic from Methods CGM description.

growth_records.strain: M150B (chemically mutagenized, directed-evolution-selected, asporogenous; spo0A 24-bp deletion)
growth_records.medium: CGM (Clostridial Growth Medium) with 100 g/L crude glycerol as sole carbon source
growth_records.cite: Sandoval, Nicholas R et al. Whole-genome sequence of an evolved Clostridium pasteurianum strain reveals Spo0A deficiency responsible for increased butanol production and superior growth. Biotechnology for Biofuels (2015). DOI:10.1186/s13068-015-0408-7. [PMC4690370]
growth_records.snippet: Cpa strain M150B showed a decreased lag phase, 65 % higher glycerol consumption (40.4 g/L), and 91 % higher butanol production (final titer 7.1 g/L) compared to the WT in 100 g/L crude glycerol. The WT strain produced more PDO than the muta
growth_records.notes: M150B crude-glycerol condition. Directly reported titers: glycerol consumed 40.4 g/L, butanol 7.1 g/L, PDO 1.5 g/L. Titers only, no time-normalized rate or numeric growth rate (specific growth rate qualitatively 'increased'; shown only in figures). Ethanol/lactate <0.5 g/L. Acetate partially consumed. Conditions (37C, pH 6.8, anaerobic batch) from Methods CGM description.

growth_records.medium: CGM (Clostridial Growth Medium) with 60 g/L pure (molecular-biology-grade) glycerol as sole carbon source

up.method: HPLC (Fig. 2b); derived from M150B value and reported ratio

growth_records.cite: Sandoval, Nicholas R et al. Whole-genome sequence of an evolved Clostridium pasteurianum strain reveals Spo0A deficiency responsible for increased butanol production and superior growth. Biotechnology for Biofuels (2015). DOI:10.1186/s13068-015-0408-7. [PMC4690370]
growth_records.snippet: While there was no lag phase for either the mutant or WT when grown on 60 g/L pure glycerol, M150B consumed 75 % more glycerol (43 g/L) and produced 80 % more butanol (11.7 g/L). As under the crude glycerol condition, the WT produced more P
growth_records.notes: WT pure-glycerol condition. Titers only. WT glycerol consumption derived: M150B 43 g/L = 75% higher => WT = 43/1.75 = 24.57 g/L. WT butanol derived: M150B 11.7 g/L = 80% higher => WT = 11.7/1.80 = 6.50 g/L. WT PDO 1.5 g/L stated directly (mutant 0.9). No time-normalized rate or numeric growth rate. Conditions from Methods CGM description.

growth_records.medium: CGM (Clostridial Growth Medium) with 60 g/L pure (molecular-biology-grade) glycerol as sole carbon source
growth_records.cite: Sandoval, Nicholas R et al. Whole-genome sequence of an evolved Clostridium pasteurianum strain reveals Spo0A deficiency responsible for increased butanol production and superior growth. Biotechnology for Biofuels (2015). DOI:10.1186/s13068-015-0408-7. [PMC4690370]
growth_records.snippet: M150B consumed 75 % more glycerol (43 g/L) and produced 80 % more butanol (11.7 g/L). As under the crude glycerol condition, the WT produced more PDO than the mutant from pure glycerol, but for both strains, less was produced overall (1.5 a
growth_records.notes: M150B pure-glycerol condition. Directly reported titers: glycerol consumed 43 g/L, butanol 11.7 g/L, PDO 0.9 g/L. Titers only, no time-normalized rate or numeric growth rate (growth 'superior' but specific growth rate shown only in figures). Ethanol/lactate <0.5 g/L. Acetate partially consumed. Conditions from Methods CGM description. NOTE: paper is primarily a genome-sequencing/mutation study; the only quantitative physiology is these batch endpoint titers.
